title: Jacket potato with prawns and Marie Rose sauce
subtitle: Crisp-baked potato with cold prawns in a creamy tomato sauce
excerpt: Prawn marie rose on a jacket potato is a slightly more grown-up version of the classic British baked potato, borrowing from the retro starter tradition. Cold prawns, a sharp marie rose sauce, and hot crisp potato skin work together rather well.
sourceNotes: Marie Rose sauce is a British invention, associated with the 1960s British restaurant scene and popularised in prawn cocktail. The jacket potato application is a natural extension of the classic starter, common in British sandwich bars and pubs.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The marie rose sauce can be made a day ahead and kept chilled.

glossaryTerms.definition: A British sauce made from mayonnaise, tomato ketchup, a dash of Worcestershire sauce, and lemon juice. Pale pink, creamy, and mildly tangy. Associated with prawn cocktail.

content.text: is a combination that feels both retro and right: the cold, creamy sauce with the slightly sweet prawns contrasts well with the hot, salty potato skin. The sauce takes 2 minutes to make and improves if made a little ahead so the flavours settle.

items.prepNote: large floury variety, about 300 g each
peeled cold-water prawns or king prawns

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Prick the potatoes, rub with oil and sea salt, and bake directly on the oven rack for 70 to 75 minutes until the skin is crisp and the flesh is soft all the way through.
Make the Marie Rose sauce and serve
Mix the mayonnaise, ketchup, Worcestershire sauce, and lemon juice in a bowl. Season with black pepper. Fold in the prawns.
Split the hot potatoes open, squeeze to open up, and spoon the prawn mixture generously over the top.

items.cause: Too much lemon juice or ketchup.
items.fix: Add a little more mayonnaise to balance.

items.cause: Cooked prawns were not drained or patted dry before mixing.
items.fix: Pat the prawns dry with kitchen paper before adding them to the sauce.

items.cause: Foil was used, or the temperature was too low.
items.fix: Bake uncovered at high heat. No foil.

content.text: Marie Rose sauce was invented in British restaurants in the 1960s. It became best known as the dressing for prawn cocktail, which appeared on nearly every British starter menu for two decades. On a jacket potato it feels nostalgic and slightly special. Cold and creamy against hot and salty is a reliable combination.
